text: The video shows a direct inguinal hernia located medial to the inferior epigastric vessels, clearly visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon creates a preperitoneal flap to expose the hernia space, beginning dissection in a sweeping motion from top to bottom. The hernia contents, consisting of preperitoneal adipose tissue, are visible protruding through the direct hernia space. The preperitoneal sac is dissected from these contents, which are then reduced back into the abdominal cavity. Cooper's ligament is exposed to ensure adequate dissection. The indirect space is evaluated, and a cord lipoma is dissected from the vas deferens and testicular vessels, with no indirect hernia present.

The surgeon deploys a self-fixating mesh into the preperitoneal space, ensuring it covers all potential hernia spaces without the need for sutures or tacks. The preperitoneal space is then sutured closed, marking the completion of the operation.
